content.textBody:
Last year I heard a friend describe spring as the season when everything comes back to life — the season of new beginnings. The more I thought about it, the more it stuck with me. The leaves return to the branches, flowers begin to bloom, and the grass turns green again. For the golfers out there, you can finally find your tee shot in the fairway without digging through fallen leaves. Everywhere you look, there are signs of new life.
In the same way this applies to the landscape, it sometimes shows up in our social circles, personal relationships, and community as well. Recently we learned that some of our closest friends will be relocating out of state — bittersweet news. We’re excited for the new chapter ahead of them, but we’re also a little sad knowing the nights out, spontaneous house drop-ins, and simply doing life together will become less frequent. It’s a reminder that we never quite know who might be stepping into a new beginning — whether it’s relocating to a new community, trying a new activity, breaking old habits, or simply starting fresh.
New beginnings often bring a sense of hope — sometimes even a nervous hope. Hope that we can rebuild what we had. Hope that we’re making the right decision. Hope that we’ll be accepted and find our place again. This season, most importantly, reminds us of the greatest hope of all — the hope we have in Jesus because He died and rose again. Because of that hope, we can trust that our new beginnings will be okay.
My encouragement this season is simple: be welcoming and be kind, because we never know who might be walking through a season of new beginnings. And if you find yourself in one, get out, be present, and experience the new life unfolding around you. Be curious. It’s not always about recreating what you had before — sometimes it’s about creating something new. In our community, in our relationships, and in our faith, spring reminds us that God is always at work, making all things new.
God Bless,
Kevin Thompson
Publisher, Life Around Senoia

3. Fertilize with Purpose
Feed your lawn based on your soil results. Balanced nutrients strengthen roots and encourage thick, healthy growth.
4. Water Deep — and Watch Drainage
Water deeply, not frequently. Deep saturation builds stronger roots, while shallow watering creates weak turf. Early morning is best. And keep an eye on drainage — standing water can be just as harmful as drought.
5. Mow Smart
Rotate mowing directions, keep blades sharp, mow at the proper height, and clean your mower regularly to prevent spreading disease.
A greener lawn isn’t about one quick fix — it’s about consistent, intentional care. Test first, treat wisely, water smart, and stay patient. Before long, that winter brown gives way

After a long Southern winter, lawns around Senoia can look a little worn — brown patches, weeds creeping in, soil that’s compacted and thirsty. The good news? With the right approach, green is closer than you think.
We talked with the team at Innovative Irrigation & Lighting about what truly matters when transitioning from dormancy to growth. Here’s your simple Spring to Green Checklist:
1. Start with a Soil Test
Before applying anything, test your soil. The Coweta County Agriculture Center or The UGA Extension Office in Spalding County can tell you exactly what your lawn truly needs. Guesswork often leads to wasted time and money.
2. Get Ahead of Weeds
Apply a pre-emergent early to prevent weeds before they start. If they’ve already popped up, use a post-emergent to eliminate competition for nutrients and water.
A Simple Guide to Bringing Your Lawn Back to Life

Graduation requirements were often just the visible symptom of deeper struggles.

What started as academic intervention has evolved into something much broader — equipping students with life skills, trade exposure, business training, and real-world opportunity.
By working directly with businesses, ELEVATE identifies workforce gaps in our own community and helps build programs that prepare students to step into those roles. Trades. Technical skills. Entrepreneurial pathways. Career readiness.
It’s no longer just about helping students graduate.
It’s about helping them thrive.

That’s where ELEVATE comes in.

Every year at dawn on Good Friday, the same question pops up across social media:
“Who are those people walking with crosses?”
The answer is simple. It’s a community Cross Walk organized by Andrew Lowe — and it’s open to anyone who would like to participate.
Andrew, originally from Belfast, Northern Ireland, moved to Senoia in 2020. A former professional rugby player, he came to the United States to continue his athletic career, eventually settling in Georgia after years of traveling back and forth to visit family. When he and his wife chose Senoia as home, Andrew says it “checked all of our boxes.”
Since then, the Lowe family has put down deep roots in the community. In 2024, Andrew was elected to Senoia’s City Council, where he continues to serve with a focus on protecting the town’s historic charm and preserving the character of downtown.
But long before public office, Andrew carried another tradition with him.

For others, it burns bright like a guiding light. But whether faint or strong, faith has the power to anchor us in seasons of change.
The resurrection of Jesus Christ is the ultimate declaration of new beginnings. From the darkest moment in history came the brightest hope the world has ever known. Through His victory over death, Jesus opened the door to a life filled with purpose, forgiveness, and a hope that cannot fade. His resurrection means our past does not define us, our failures do not imprison us, and our future is held securely in His hands.
This spring, as new life emerges all around us, may our hearts awaken too. May we receive the fresh hope that Jesus offers—a hope that renews our faith,

With the shift in seasons, our communities come alive as well. People step outdoors again, filling yards and parks with laughter and conversation. Friendships deepen, new connections form, and we find ourselves breathing a little easier, reminded of life’s simple gifts.
These rhythms of renewal are more than just the turning of a calendar page. They speak to a deeper truth: within every season of life, God offers us the opportunity to begin again. Where we see endings, He sees potential. Where we feel stuck, He brings freedom. Where hope seems lost, He breathes it back into our hearts.

For many, hope can feel like something always just out of reach—longed for but never fully held. It often appears to belong to others, those who seem to have life figured out, while we stand at a distance wondering if hope could ever be meant for us. After the long stillness of winter, the world reawakens. Trees stretch new buds toward the sun, flowers push through once frozen soil, and color returns to a landscape that felt asleep. Even the pollen—though it covers everything in sight, especially here in the South—is a signal that life is stirring again. Nature becomes a canvas of renewal, whispering to our hearts that beginnings are still possible.

content.textBody:
Towering above the garden is perhaps the property’s most extraordinary natural feature: a 350-year-old Orange Osage tree facing Broad Street. Believed to be the third-largest specimen of its kind in the United States, the tree predates the house itself by nearly two centuries. Its sweeping limbs serve as both a natural landmark and a reminder of the deep roots that run through Senoia’s landscape.
The Kings also looked beyond Georgia for architectural inspiration when enhancing the property’s exterior. The brick wall along Broad Street draws influence from the historic estates of Charleston, South Carolina, where brick garden walls provide privacy and timeless character. Anchoring the entrance are custom metal gates featuring welded scabbards on each side, inspired by Charleston’s famous Sword Gate House, a historic property commissioned by Abraham Lincoln’s granddaughter.
Inside the home, historic character blends comfortably with modern amenities. One of the most unique spaces is the restored Parson’s Room, once associated with clergy who occupied the home in its early years. Today, the room has been thoughtfully reimagined with a loft half bath and a home theatre featuring Sonos surround sound, creating a gathering place where history and modern entertainment meet.
Altogether, the home offers 4 bedrooms, 4 full bathrooms, and 2 half bathrooms across 3,222 square feet, along with the Parson’s Room and a bonus room for added flexibility. Located squarely within Senoia’s historic downtown district, the property also carries existing commercial zoning, opening possibilities that range from residential living to boutique commercial use.
The Tilted House is more than simply a restored structure—it is a living story of craftsmanship, landscape, and community. Through the careful work of the Kings, a home that once leaned quietly through the decades now stands as one of Senoia’s most distinctive historic treasures.

Among the home’s most fascinating treasures are five fireplaces, each carefully restored using the geometry described in The Lost Art of the 18th Century Fireplace by Vrest Orton. This historic design method focuses on precise proportions within the firebox and smoke chamber to improve airflow and efficiency. The fireplaces not only serve as architectural centerpieces, they also revive a nearly forgotten art of early American hearth construction.
The flooring beneath your feet carries an even older story. Much of the home features old-growth heart pine harvested in the late 1700s and sourced from Mississippi. Dense, richly colored, and impossible to replicate with modern lumber, the wood adds warmth and the occasional glimpse of cottonseed oil revealing itself from the antique wood when the sun shines through the sole plate glass window in the master suite.
Step outside and the grounds themselves feel like a quiet Southern garden retreat. More than 50 plantings-including azaleas, antique azaleas, gardenias, climbing roses, mature hollies, snowflake camellias, and Chinese fringe trees create a landscape that blooms through much of the year and frames the property with color and fragrance.

The Tilted House:
Gateway to Town

In the heart of historic downtown Senoia, along Broad Street, sits the “1871 Tilted House.” Like many buildings from the Reconstruction era, time left its subtle marks on the structure, giving the home its nickname. Today, however, the Tilted House stands stronger than ever thanks to a meticulous modernization/restoration project that honors its past while preparing it for the future.
The project was undertaken by Senoia residents Skip and Cindy King, who have developed a quiet reputation for restoring historic properties with a careful balance of craftsmanship and respect for architectural heritage. Rather than simply renovate old homes, the Kings approach each project as a stewardship of history-preserving the details that give a building its soul while thoughtfully updating it for modern life.
The Tilted House itself dates to 1871, built in a region still emerging from the aftermath of the Civil War. Like many homes of that period, it was constructed with materials and techniques that valued both function and beauty. Some of the homes original wallpaper can be viewed at the Senoia Area Historical Society Museum on Couch Street.

April 11th - Songs of the South - A Tribute to Alabama
Location: Lake Marimac, 148 Pylant Street, Senoia GA 30276
Dust off your boots and get ready for a good ole’ Southern night as Songs of the South - An Alabama Tribute Band, rolls into Senoia, GA! Get ready for an evening of small town vibes and sing along to those classic Alabama hits that we all know by heart. Whether you’re reliving the soundtrack of “Mountain Music,” reminiscing about “Dixieland Delight,” or simply enjoying a night out with great live music, this concert promises a memorable Southern experience in the heart of Senoia.
Please note, this is a 21 and over event. Valid identification will be required for entry.

April 26th - Taste of Senoia!
Location: Senoia’s Farmer’s Market
Come savor the flavor at Taste of Senoia, an annual event hosted by the Senoia Optimist Club!
Purchase your tickets and stroll through Senoia while sampling delicious small bites from a variety of local restaurants. It’s a fun-filled day of food, friends, and community as you enjoy the very best “tastes” our town has to offer. Restaurants will be serving on Main Street, Barnes Street, and at Senoia Farmers' Market!
All proceeds benefit the Senoia Optimist Club, a nonprofit organization dedicated to supporting families in our community with a special focus on helping children. For questions about the event please email senoiaoptimistclub@gmail.com

May 1st - Summer Boutique Crawl
Location: Downtown Senoia
Join us for the Summer Crawl, a fun-filled day of shopping local, sipping speciality drinks, and strolling around Downtown Senoia! Participating boutiques will be offering exclusive discounts, giveaways, sip & shop experiences, and more!
You’ll receive a Crawl Card to get stamped at each stop you shop at. Complete your card and turn it in for a chance to win the Ultimate Senoia Grand Prize! With a live DJ setting the vibe, great company, and Mother’s Day right around the corner, this is your sign to grab your girlfriends, shop small, and make a day of it in Senoia!

The pleats, insertions, and structure suggest the dress was likely made by a skilled dressmaker, possibly even locally. And once worn? It would have required laundering, starching, and careful ironing — no wrinkle-release spray in sight.
Lillie Hollberg had reason to keep up with fashion. Her husband, C.F. Hollberg, built the Hollberg Hotel (now the Veranda Historic Inn), opened multiple businesses — including Hollberg Fine Furniture, which still operates today — and served three terms as Senoia’s mayor. She was part of a family shaping the town’s growth, and her wardrobe reflected that presence.
Today’s spring dresses may prioritize comfort over corsets, and our collars don’t require bone supports to stand upright. We don’t cook starch on the stove or press pleats for hours before an event. But in many ways, spring fashion still carries a similar thread — a desire to show up well, to gather, to celebrate the season.
This dress isn’t just fabric and lace. It’s a snapshot of a woman, a family, and a town in motion — a reminder

— created by a restrictive corset, padded shaping, and a bodice so full it was known as a “monobosom.” The exaggerated leg-of-mutton sleeves of the 1890s had softened into bishop sleeves, still full but flowing more gracefully. Fashion in this era wasn’t about comfort. It was about presentation.
While most formal dresses were made of silk or wool, this one is made of white cotton — what was known at the time as a “lingerie dress.” These were worn to afternoon lawn parties, receptions, and teas. Think garden gatherings at the newly built Hollberg Hotel (1906), not tennis matches. In fact, the slight train — called a “sweep” or “brush” — suggests this dress was likely worn indoors, possibly in one of Senoia’s most prominent social settings.
The craftsmanship tells its own story. The lace adorning the dress is Leavers lace — produced on one of the most

Spring in Senoia means sundresses, patio dinners, farmers markets, and maybe a light sweater for that one last cool snap we didn’t see coming. Today’s spring style is effortless — breathable fabrics, soft silhouettes, and dresses you can throw on for brunch, church, or an evening stroll down Main Street.
But step inside the Senoia Area Historical Society Museum on Couch Street, and you’ll find a spring dress that tells a very different story.
On display is a white cotton dress worn by Lillian “Lillie” Sibley Hollberg during the first decade of the 1900s. At first glance, it looks delicate and refined — lace details, sheer sleeves, and a high collar that commands attention. But this wasn’t just a dress. It was a statement.

content.textBody:
Happy beginning of fall, everyone! I hope your summer has been filled with opportunities to make memories…maybe through travel or learning to play Mahjong with new friends or simply having a chat with a neighbor in the front yard. I often feel so lucky to know so many amazing and interesting folks in Canyon Creek, first through co-owning Sweet Firefly and now by sharing your stories here. 
My husband Trey and I discovered the neighborhood because a Canyon Creek resident, Erica Yaeger, used to make and sell the most amazing baby blankets, one of which we received at a baby shower for our newborn son. I knew I had to make these monogrammed, perfect-for-swaddling treasures my go-to gift for my friends’ babies. At the time we were living in Addison but looking for a neighborhood with swings and slides, trees and sidewalks. Who knew that there was a neighborhood tucked away in Richardson that fit the bill? 
It took us three years to get here, but I’m so glad we did. And people keep finding our hidden gem. Entrepreneur Abby Lane chose II Creeks as the location for Chérie Lane, our business of the month and her flagship storefront boutique, which opened last month. Julie and Mike Hughes, our spotlighted family in this issue, fell in love in England and found their way to their beloved Sage Valley home two decades ago. 
Georgia Nelson walks Murphy, her precious bordoodle, down High Brook and Canyon Brook daily and always stops to greet her neighbors. You’ll meet Georgia and Murphy in these pages. And Pearce senior Will Miltenberger, whom I had the privilege of watching grow up through his frequent visits to Sweet Firefly with his family, is our student athlete of the month. What a wonderful young man he has turned out to be.
One of the best ways to meet your neighbors is at our National Night Out celebrations happening this year in October. We’ve asked Sharon Odell, our CCHOA Crime Watch Director since 2021, to share her story and to let you know all about the upcoming NNO activities.
I just know there’s someone out there reading this letter who would love to be featured in the magazine. Don’t be shy about reaching out and letting us know! We want to meet you and look forward to sharing your unique story. 

Patti Otte

content.textBody:
4. Upgrade the Flooring
Replace standard concrete with natural stone, brick pavers, stamped concrete, or composite decking. These options not only elevate the look of your patio but also improve durability and slip resistance. Heated flooring systems can even extend your outdoor season in cooler climates.

5. Create Multi-Zone Layouts
Think beyond a single seating area. Divide your patio into zones—a dining space, a lounge area, and a cooking station—to make it more functional. Use planters, low walls, or changes in flooring texture to subtly define each section.

6. Install Outdoor Heating or Cooling Features
Ceiling fans, misting systems, and infrared heaters can make your patio comfortable in almost any weather. These features can be built into overhead structures for a sleek, integrated look.

7. Enhance with Landscaping
Tie your patio into the rest of your yard with custom landscaping. Built-in planters, raised garden beds, and decorative retaining walls create a seamless transition between hardscape and greenery.

With these remodeling upgrades, your patio can go from simple to spectacular—offering a versatile, year-round outdoor

Your patio is more than a place to sit—it can be an extension of your home’s living and entertaining areas. With the right upgrades, you can create a space that blends style, comfort, and functionality, while adding long-term value to your property. Here are some remodeling ideas to give your patio a whole new life.

1. Build an Outdoor Kitchen
An outdoor kitchen takes entertaining to the next level. Install a built-in grill, countertop prep space, storage cabinets, and even a sink or refrigerator. Add a bar counter with stools so guests can relax while you cook. Durable materials like stainless steel, stone, and tile keep it looking great for years.

2. Add a Fireplace or Fire Pit
A fireplace can become the focal point of your patio, creating warmth and ambiance for year-round enjoyment. For a more casual setup, consider a built-in stone fire pit with surrounding seating. Both options make cooler evenings more inviting and add a cozy charm to the space.

3. Incorporate a Pergola or Covered Structure
Adding a pergola, pavilion, or full roof cover extends your use of the patio in hot or rainy weather. Pair it with retractable shades or ceiling fans to improve comfort. If you love to entertain, you can also integrate built-in lighting and speakers into the structure.

I would start with a morning hunt with my good friend Lucas Thomas (lives in Canyon Creek) and then relaxing for a bit. I’d have a good Shadys lunch, then enjoy a round of golf at Canyon Creek Country Club and end the day with Dos Arroyos enchiladas for dinner.

Honest, determined, motivated

The brotherhood and bonds I have created through football are very special to me. I’ve always felt at home playing football, especially at a young age. He explained to me that I can do special things within the program and that I am a special leader; I have trusted him ever since.

Consider the following to help avoid becoming a victim of this type of insurance fraud. Ghost brokers may apply for a genuine policy in your name, keep your premium payments, then cancel the policy shortly after — all without your knowledge. This practice allows the fraudsters to deceive unsuspecting "customers" into thinking they have a legitimate policy. The victim may only find out that the policy was canceled after they have to make an insurance claim.
Ghost brokers typically advertise cheap insurance on social media sites

Scammers have been known to pose as insurance agents selling cheap, fraudulent policies to consumers who think they're obtaining legitimate coverage from a licensed insurer. Victims may not discover they've been scammed until they file a claim — only to be told that their policy is not in force with the company.
Doing business with these false insurance agents, sometimes called "ghost brokers," could put you, your family and your finances at risk.

content.textBody:
The word BEST, by definition, means: that which is the most excellent, outstanding, or desirable. 

RICHARDSON: a city in Texas boasting a population of close to 120,000 people, is the city we call home. 

BEST OF RICHARDSON: a contest compromised of businesses, organizations, people, events and locations throughout the city that is voted on by readers and members of the community, representing all that makes Richardson the best place to call home.

What’s exciting about the Best of Richardson awards is that being named the Best of Richardson in any category is a prestigious honor. If you followed along this voting journey, you know it’s been a methodical process of gathering all the names of the beloved people, places and businesses around Richardson during the April nomination phase. In this first round, the readers of Richardson Life Magazine showed up with more than 950 nominations in 200+ categories—amazing! 

Then, as the contest narrowed, the top three nominees with the most nominations in each category advanced to the finalist voting round in June. That round, you cast more than 50,000 votes to get us to today’s announcement of the winners by category. The moment so many have been waiting for. The BEST of Richardson. 

Thank you, Richardson Life Magazine readers, for showing up to support your community. And thank you to all the businesses and individuals for taking part and rallying your supporters. There are so many unbelievable places here to see and experience. I hope you’ll also check out our online directory, which includes not only the winners in each category, but the finalists as well. 

We’ll be back next year with another edition of Best of Richardson. If you don’t see one of your favorites listed, you can always nominate them next year to bring them to the winner’s circle.

We believe Richardson is the absolute BEST place to live.

Happy August! If you’ve been a regular reader of Richardson Life Magazine, you’ll notice that this issue looks a little—actually, a lot—different than the usual. Some of our normally featured articles are absent and the calendar of events and Scene section are missing. They’ll be back in future issues. Instead, the pages of this Best of Richardson issue are dedicated to the companies, individuals and organizations you all voted as 2025 stand-outs. 

In spite of the temporary changes, you’ll still get to hear from our new mayor, Amir Omar, about his first ninety days in office. And you’ll see how RISD kicked off the school year by celebrating its teachers and offering hands-on training to prepare them for the school year. They truly are the BEST school district, thanks to Superintendent Tabitha Branum’s leadership, her administrative team and all the teachers, counselors, principals, culinary team members, support staff and others who make RISD run. In spite of recent budget cuts, Branum and her team continue to deliver a top-notch education to Richardson’s students.

And although we may not have a calendar in this issue, I’d like to point out a couple of important upcoming events. On August 28 at 7:00 pm, the 15th annual Richardson Real Heroes Awards Celebration, sponsored by the City of Richardson, will take place at the Eisemann Center’s Bank of America Theatre. 2025 award recipients Dr. Mona Alnaeemi and Neil Stampe will be honored for their outstanding volunteer service to the Richardson community, along with the 2025 nominees. The awards ceremony’s special guest will be Texas-based singer/songwriter/speaker Sara Hickman, who will weave song into her talk about the power of giving back.  Tickets are free but must be obtained from the Eisemann Center Box Office. Details can be found at the RRH website at richardsonrealheroes.com/awardsceremony.  

Additionally, Network is hosting its 28th Annual Golf Outing on Friday, September 12, at Sherrill Park. If you’re a golfer and want to support an amazing cause, sign up today at thenetwork.org/golf. Network continues to meet the needs of our community in large ways and small and needs our support now more than ever. If you’re not a golfer, visit Network’s website to see how your gifts and talents align with their mission.

Aren’t we lucky to live in Richardson? I know I am!

How Richardson Real Estate 
Became the Best

When people first moved to Richardson decades ago, it was all about family-friendly neighborhoods, top-rated schools, and a quieter pace of life just outside the Dallas hustle. As local realtors, we’ve watched the city evolve without losing the qualities that made it special in the first place.

Then: A Suburban Gem
In the 1970s and ’80s, Richardson was a hidden gem—safe, suburban, and community-oriented. Young families were drawn in by strong RISD schools, affordable homes, and the promise of a quality life. Homes ranged from $40,000 to $80,000, with established neighborhoods like Canyon Creek and Greenwood Hills offering charm and space. The nearby Telecom Corridor made Richardson an ideal spot for professionals working in tech and engineering.

RLM: What’s something most people don’t know about you that helps shape how you lead?
I’m naturally very analytical—I like to gather a lot of input before making decisions. Research data, feedback from residents, insights from staff, all of it. I try to look at an issue from every angle, a full 360-degrees. It’s not about overthinking—it’s about making sure decisions are grounded, balanced and built to last. I’ve found the more perspectives we include early on, the better the outcome is for everyone involved.

RLM: What’s one goal you’ve set for yourself since taking office?
To stay accessible and to lead with consistency in words, tone and actions—because in a city of our size, people pay very close attention. I want residents to feel confident that they know where I stand, that I’ll follow through and that I’m working every single day to earn their trust. I’ve been thrilled about the monthly coffees I’ve held and for the upcoming quarterly town hall I’m hosting. The input I gather and the ability to connect with residents, helps me stay aligned with everyone I represent and their vision for the City of Richardson.

RLM: What do you see as Richardson’s biggest opportunity?
One of the biggest is how we respond to shifts in the commercial real estate landscape. Proactively reimagining those spaces, supporting innovation, guiding redevelopment that aligns with long-term resident priorities—all of it will improve the outcome. Richardson’s commercial tax base is a key element in our overall financial health. Leveraging our strengths and recent successes will accelerate momentum and, ultimately, the results will be positive.

many of which have resulted in multiple recommendations of retailers, businesses and restaurants that I am in contact with to help fill spaces. And I’ve recently spoken to a renowned artist within our very own city to begin a conversation about using one of his works as a mural to truly elevate our entry point. 

RLM: What’s one small but meaningful win you’ve experienced since taking office?
I am proud that this Council and our excellent city management team were willing to live-stream and record our goal-setting workshop, something that hadn’t happened since I was on the Council in 2011. These goals, strategies and tactics shape much of what our focus will be during our two-year City Council terms and it’s important that our residents are able to watch consequential meetings like this live, or to be able to go back days—or even years—later to see what was discussed and the thinking behind them. Transparency builds trust and this is a step in the right direction.

RLM: What’s been the most challenging part of the job so far?
Managing time and energy. The demand is constant—and I mean, constant. Every moment is a chance to listen, solve or lead. I’m learning to pace myself—to be fully present for each conversation while keeping the big picture in view.

Education That Inspires 
at Every Level
Education is a cornerstone of life in Richardson. We benefit from access to high-quality public education, with the city served by two highly-respected school districts: Richardson ISD and Plano ISD. These districts are known for strong academics, award-winning fine arts programs and competitive extracurricular opportunities. Students have access to specialized magnet schools, STEM initiatives and language immersion programs that prepare them for a global future. 

Beyond K-12, Richardson is also home to The University of Texas at Dallas (UTD), a nationally celebrated research institution with a growing arts presence and its technological influence can be felt throughout the city’s innovation-driven economy. Additionally, Dallas College's Richland Campus offers affordable, accessible pathways for workforce

A Community Rich in Diversity
Richardson’s cultural diversity is evident in its neighborhoods, shops, community events and nonprofits. The city’s dining options alone reflect this global influence. You’ll find authentic Ethiopian, Korean, Asian and Indian cuisine—all within blocks of each other. More than 80 languages are spoken in area schools and the city is home to people from every corner of the globe. 

Global grocery stores, colorful murals and multicultural celebrations make Richardson feel both vibrant and inclusive. From the City-sponsored annual Culture in the Core event to the Dialogue Institute’s Interfaith Thanksgiving celebrations and the Chinese Lunar New Year Festival, Richardson boasts a variety of opportunities for residents and visitors to learn more about each other and increase their cultural understanding and embrace.

into a locally owned shop where the barista knows your order, the owner chats with you by name, and every detail feels personal. Richardson’s small business community is filled with passion, personality and a whole lot of heart.

Take Bella Flan, for example—a local restaurant known for its authentic Cuban sandwiches, burgers and desserts. La Casita Bakeshop, with its nationally recognized pastries, serves everything from hand-laminated croissants to pillowy beignets and delicious loaves of sourdough bread. You’ll also find local shopping spots like Beyond the Door, Lone Chimney Mercantile, Beaucoup, Get Noticed and Cottonwood Market in various parts of the city. And Del’s Charcoal Burgers, a no-frills staple, has been feeding generations since 1957. These businesses—and many others—help preserve the authentic, neighborly feel that defines Richardson.

Outdoor Spaces That Invite Exploration and Community Gatherings
We love spending time outside in Richardson! Its 1,000+ acres of parks and green spaces, along with more than 40 miles of hike-and-bike trails winding through wooded areas, creeks and neighborhoods, invite all of us to spend more time outdoors. Spring Creek Nature Area and Breckenridge Park are perfect for walking, jogging, or simply enjoying a quiet afternoon in nature. And one of the city’s most picturesque spots is Prairie Creek Park, where a hidden waterfall draws families, photographers and nature lovers alike. It’s even recently been discovered by Instagram users, much to the surprise of locals who live near the picturesque park. 

Local shopping venues even offer beautiful outdoor gathering spaces. From the quaint II Creeks Shopping Center in the heart of Canyon Creek to CityLine’s center to the newly revitalized downtown Richardson, residents can spend time enjoying events in these places or simply have a place to stop for a bit while spending an afternoon at local favorites.

content.textBody:
This year, more than 50,000 votes were cast for the Best of Richardson. The enthusiasm was electric and the wide range of categories showed just how diverse and vibrant our city really is. And while the recognition is great, the real reward is knowing that the community sees you, appreciates you, and wants to say, “Thank you.”
The profiles on the following pages spotlight the winners across all categories. You’ll find winners from the small, family-run businesses that have been staples for decades, to the rising stars and the new favorites. As you glance through the profiles, you’ll see why they were nominated, how they stand out, and why Richardson is better because they’re part of it.
We hope you’ll take the time to not only read their stories, but to visit them, support them, and tell them thanks. These winners earned the BEST of Richardson award through community trust, hard work, heart…and your votes!
So from all of us at Richardson Life, congratulations to the winners. And to everyone who voted, thank you for making Best of Richardson a genuine reflection of who we are as a city. The best city around.

The Best of Richardson readers choice awards was created to recognize the standout businesses, services, and individuals that make life in our city better. But what sets this contest apart is how authentic and organic it truly is. This isn’t a popularity contest driven by our staff; it’s a celebration powered by you, the people who take pride in the place you call home. 
When the team here at Richardson Life Magazine started discussing the idea of creating a Best of Richardson contest early last year, it was quickly decided that it should be an award voted on by the readers. That is when an independent third party was contracted to ensure fairness and transparency with the voting process. Strict voting rules were put in place to prevent duplicate entries or ballot-stuffing, and results were carefully reviewed to maintain the integrity of the contest. Whether you voted once or rallied your community to support your favorites, you were part of something real! It’s a local lovefest for the best of the best.
What’s even more exciting is that these winners aren’t just logos or storefronts. They’re your neighbors, your friends, your kid’s coach, or that one barista who remembers your latte order. They’re part of the everyday fabric of Richardson. These are people who pour their hearts into what they do, not just for profit, but for pride in their craft and in their community.
Voted on By the People, For the Community

content.textBody: Join Us: Take Your Place In The Shade

In 2013, five guys rooted in the Richardson, Texas, neighborhood of Canyon Creek, came together one evening in support of a single cause; give family, friends, and neighbors a unique place to hang out and share some tasty burgers and beer – a real neighborhood joint.
The original plan was simple; offer great food at a reasonable price in a casual, family-friendly atmosphere. We’ve caused a bit of a brewhaha since then with our one-of-a-kind burgers, but the mission has always stayed the same. So, whether it’s your first time here, or you’re someone we like to call neighbor, it’s with pride that we welcome you to Shady’s
From the local beer on tap, to our from scratch kitchen, Shady’s was built out of a growing desire to serve the community. A local spot, created for you and yours to enjoy great food without all the fuss. We hope you like it!
Scratch Kitchen. Craft Cocktails. Killer Patio.
Winning Best Fine Dining, Romantic Restaurant, and Upscale Bar is no surprise—it’s where style, flavor, and community meet. Feed your need for fresh. Feed your soul. Only at The Fifth.

Best Fine Dining Restaurant
Best Romantic Restaurant
Best Upscale Bar

At The Fifth, you don’t just dine—you experience. This modern-rustic gem in the heart of Richardson has quickly become a local favorite for its bold flavors, laid-back charm, and elevated atmosphere. With a scratch kitchen focused on fresh, seasonal ingredients, each visit delivers something new—from tender Pan-Seared Salmon and Smoked Short Rib to creative pastas and refined comfort classics. Gluten-free? No problem—just ask.
 But what truly sets The Fifth apart is its vibe. The Fireside Patio Bar, complete with a glowing central fireplace and all-season breezes, is the go-to hangout for brunches, cocktails, and live music under the stars. Whether you’re enjoying a date night, catching up with friends, or celebrating something special, The Fifth makes every moment feel elevated.
